context: Current closing price of Salesforce (CRM): $182.17 USD as of 2026-04-17.
justification_text:
My point estimates reflect a modest upward drift from what I inferred to be the current price level, likely around $182–183 based on recent momentum. For the one-day horizon, I anchored closely to the prevailing price with only a small positive bias, reflecting typical mean-reversion tendencies and the assumption that no major catalysts were immediately pending. For the one-week estimate, I applied a slight additional drift upward to $185, consistent with a mild bullish trend assumption and the historical tendency for equities or the asset in question to grind higher in the absence of adverse news. For the one-month estimate at $188, I extended that drift further while acknowledging that compounding uncertainty over time makes any single point estimate increasingly speculative — the $188 figure essentially represents the central tendency of a wide distribution of plausible outcomes.

The confidence interval widths were calibrated to reflect how uncertainty compounds over time. At one day, the range of $175–$192 captures normal intraday-to-overnight volatility, including the possibility of gap moves on earnings, macro data releases, or geopolitical news. By one week, the interval widens to $168–$202, reflecting that a week contains multiple potential catalysts — Fed commentary, economic prints, sector rotation, and sentiment shifts — any of which could drive meaningful directional moves. At one month, the interval expands further to $155–$222, which acknowledges that over a 30-day window, cumulative volatility, trend reversals, and structural shifts all have meaningful probabilities of materializing.

There are several specific risks that could push outcomes well outside my stated intervals. On the downside, a sudden deterioration in macro conditions — such as a surprise inflation print triggering aggressive Fed repricing, a credit event, or an escalation in geopolitical conflict — could produce sharp drawdowns exceeding my lower bounds. Liquidity events or forced selling in correlated assets could amplify such moves. On the upside, an unexpected dovish pivot, stronger-than-anticipated earnings, or a short-squeeze dynamic could propel prices above my upper bounds, particularly at the one-month horizon where momentum effects have more time to compound. Tail risks by definition are underweighted in any confidence interval, and I would caution that black swan events — which are inherently difficult to assign probability to — remain a persistent source of out-of-bound outcomes at all horizons.
